name: Volund
description: Volund, also known as Wayland the Smith, is a prominent figure in Norse mythology, renowned as a master smith and craftsman. His story, primarily recounted in the *Völundarkviða* of the *Poetic Edda* and elaborated upon in the *Þiðreks saga*, is a tragic narrative of enslavement and revenge. Volund's exceptional skills allowed him to create intricate and powerful artifacts, making him a highly sought-after artisan. He is frequently associated with elves and dwarves, reflecting his mastery of metalworking techniques considered almost supernatural. His tale explores themes of artistic genius, the abuse of power, and the lengths to which one will go to seek retribution for grievous wrongs. The motif of forced labor and the loss of freedom resonate throughout his myth, making Volund a complex and compelling character within the broader Norse mythological landscape. His legacy persists in place names and artistic representations, testifying to his enduring cultural significance.
subtitle: Master Smith of the Elves

keyMyths.title: Volund's Capture and Hamstringing
keyMyths.description: King Níðuðr, envious of Volund's skill, captures him and hamstrings him to prevent his escape, forcing him to work as his smith. He is imprisoned on an island and compelled to forge treasures for the king and his family. The act of hamstringing is particularly significant, as it permanently disables Volund, highlighting the brutal nature of his enslavement and fueling his desire for vengeance. This act of cruelty sets the stage for Volund's elaborate and gruesome revenge.

keyMyths.title: Volund's Revenge
keyMyths.description: While enslaved, Volund exacts a terrible revenge on Níðuðr. He kills the king's sons and fashions goblets from their skulls, which he then presents to the king. He also creates jewelry from their eyes and a brooch from their teeth, which he gives to the queen. He then impregnates the king's daughter, Böðvildr, after she brings him her broken ring to be repaired. This act is a calculated humiliation of the king and a means of perpetuating his line with Volund's own offspring.

keyMyths.title: Volund's Escape
keyMyths.description: After completing his revenge, Volund uses the feathers of the birds he has captured to fashion wings for himself. He then flies away from the island, taunting King Níðuðr with the details of his gruesome revenge. This escape symbolizes Volund's triumph over his oppressor and his restoration of freedom. The act of flying also elevates him to a semi-divine status, further cementing his legendary status as a master craftsman and avenger. His final words to the king solidify his victory.

sources.author: Larrington, Carolyne
sources.description: A modern translation and commentary on the Poetic Edda, including the Völundarkviða.
sources.text: A modern translation and commentary on the Poetic Edda, including the Völundarkviða.

sources.text: Translation of the Thidreks saga, which contains a version of the Volund story.

sources.author: Ellis Davidson, H.R.
sources.work: Gods and Myths of Northern Europe
sources.description: Provides a general overview of Norse mythology, including a discussion of Volund's role and significance.
